topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to mock me. A C-minus. After all the late nights, the meticulous research, the sheer volume of effort poured into that history paper, this was the reward? I stared at the grade as if it were a physical assault. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded with a sudden, hot tension. "This is absurd," I muttered under my breath, the sound barely audible over the rustling of papers in the quiet library corner. I snatched the graded exam back, crumpling the corner with unnecessary force before smoothing it out again, a frantic, almost violent gesture. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but the silence of the study hall pressed the air out of my lungs.

[story 2]

My hands were shaking as I reread the feedback. 'Lacks critical synthesis.' Such a vague, dismissive comment for the sprawling argument I had constructed. I slammed my textbook shut, the sound echoing sharply in the lecture hall, making a few nearby students glance over. I felt a deep, simmering resentment bubble up inside me, hot and prickly. I stood up abruptly, pushing my chair back with a screech that drew more attention. "I followed every guideline," I snapped, my voice louder than I intended, a sharp edge cutting through the ambient noise. I didn't wait for a response, just spun on my heel and walked toward the exit, the weight of the disappointing mark dragging at my shoulders.

[story 3]

I traced the margin of the assignment with a fingertip, my gaze fixed on the low score. It felt like a deliberate slight, a profound misunderstanding of the work I had presented. A slow burn started in my chest, a feeling of being utterly overlooked, of having my dedication completely undervalued. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly across my chest, my gaze distant as if looking at something miles away. When the professor finally called attendance, I met his eyes, and I allowed a flicker of pure, unadulterated frustration to cross my face—a tight set to my mouth, a stiff posture that screamed that I was deeply dissatisfied with the outcome.
